summary: Today's big story is happening right in Reynoldstown, where that long-stalled Beltline project is finally breaking ground after five years. Plus Inman Park just got a new Asian spot with soft shell crab bao, while Decatur's scoring some affordable infill housing.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Long-Stalled Beltline Project Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After sitting empty for nearly five years, that cleared corner where Chester Avenue meets Fulton Terrace is finally seeing some action. Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers to build 85 residential units just a block from the Eastside Trail. It's been a long wait for neighbors who've been staring at that vacant lot since 2021.
topStory.body:
You know that empty corner in Reynoldstown where Chester meets Fulton Terrace, just north of Memorial Drive? The one that's been sitting there cleared and lonely for almost five years now? Well, finally some good news. Embry Development confirmed they've started groundwork on what will become 85 townhomes and condos about a block west of the Beltline's Eastside Trail.

This has been a long saga for the neighborhood. Toll Brothers originally cleared the site and got permits, but then handed it off to Atlanta-based Embry Development two years ago. The 2-acre site has been a daily reminder to neighbors of what happens when development plans stall out. But now concrete trucks and construction crews signal that something's actually happening.

The project sits in that sweet spot where Reynoldstown meets the Beltline corridor, close enough to walk to Krog Street Market but still retaining some of that residential feel that longtime neighbors appreciate. With 85 units planned, it'll be interesting to see how this changes the character of this particular corner of the neighborhood.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment and cleared land at the Reynoldstown development site

This has been a long saga for the neighborhood. Toll Brothers originally cleared the site and got permits, but then handed it off to Atlanta-based Embry Development two years ago. stories.imageAlt: Construction equipment and cleared land at the Reynoldstown development site

stories.headline: Decatur's Missing Middle Housing Village Almost Ready to Launch
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is bringing 18 standalone houses to unincorporated Decatur, designed around shared courtyards and green space. The goal is creating more accessible ITP housing where median home values have climbed past $700,000. Marketing starts next week for this village-style community on Midway Road.
stories.body:
With Decatur home prices pushing past $700,000, finding anything affordable ITP feels impossible these days. But Cottages at Midway might offer some relief when it launches next week. This 18-house development on Midway Road is designed as a 'pocket neighborhood' with shared courtyards and greenspace, replacing two underused lots with what developers call 'missing middle' housing.

The village concept is pretty smart. Instead of maximizing density with big apartment blocks, they're building standalone houses that feel more residential while still being more affordable than typical Decatur real estate. The shared courtyards and common areas are designed to foster that front-porch community feeling that makes Decatur special.

At 2.1 acres total, it's not huge, but it shows how creative infill can work in established neighborhoods. The project sits close enough to downtown Decatur and major commercial corridors to be walkable, which is increasingly rare for anything approaching affordability ITP. If this model works, expect to see more pocket neighborhoods popping up around metro Atlanta.
stories.imageAlt: Site map showing the layout of Cottages at Midway development

stories.headline: West End's Beltline Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is bringing 23 for-sale townhomes to the West End along the Beltline. Developer Ackerman & Co., which also owns Lee + White, started construction a year ago and the first seven units are almost ready. It's a rare chance to buy instead of rent in this hot Beltline stretch.
stories.body:
It's not often you see for-sale townhomes going up along the Beltline these days, especially in the West End. But that's exactly what's happening at GW Parkside (they renamed it from the much clunkier 'Hopkins and White Townhomes'). Ackerman & Co. has been working on this 23-unit project for about a year now, and the first seven are almost ready to hit the market this spring.

The timing is interesting because the West End has been on fire lately, but most of what's being built is rental. Having the option to actually buy something this close to the Beltline feels refreshing. Ackerman clearly knows what they're doing in this area. They've been killing it with Lee + White and all its food and office offerings.

The location on Hopkins Street puts you within walking distance of the Westside Trail, but still in a neighborhood that feels residential rather than touristy. As the Beltline continues to mature, spots like this where you can own instead of rent are going to be increasingly valuable. The question is whether they'll price them for actual humans or just investors.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of the GW Parkside townhome development

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Soft Shell Crab Bao to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chef duo Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. The Asian-American comfort food spot serves everything from crab Rangoon puffs to Peking chicken platters, plus cocktails like Thai tea martinis. After years of pop-ups, they finally have a permanent home.
stories.body:
After watching Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez do pop-ups around town, it's exciting to see them finally get their own space. Mushi Ni opened today in Inman Park on Elizabeth Street, just steps from the Freedom Park Trail. Both chefs have serious credentials (Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in NYC), and their Asian-American comfort food concept feels perfectly suited for the neighborhood.

The menu hits all the right notes: soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, and those Peking chicken platters that feed two people for $25. They're doing lunch and dinner with a full cocktail program, which means you can grab a Thai tea martini with your crab Rangoon puffs. The location is smart too, right on that stretch where Inman Park meets the trail system.

This feels like exactly the kind of independently owned, chef-driven spot that makes Inman Park special. After years of seeing chains creep into every neighborhood, it's refreshing when actual local talent gets to open something personal and creative. Plus, being right off Freedom Park Trail means easy access whether you're biking in from Little Five or walking over from Candler Park.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue's Texas Smokehouse Lands at Ansley Mall
stories.summary: John Lewis brought his acclaimed Austin barbecue to the fancy new Ansley Mall development behind The Cook's Warehouse. The upstairs smokehouse has panoramic windows overlooking the Beltline pedestrian bridge, with six 22-foot smokers running 24/7. It's some of the best brisket you'll find in Atlanta, with a view to match.
stories.body:
John Lewis has done something pretty spectacular at Ansley Mall. His new barbecue spot doesn't hide the smokers in some back room like most places. Instead, he put six massive 22-foot smokers (welded from old propane tanks) in a loft space with floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking that new pedestrian bridge to the Beltline. It looks like the fanciest engine room in Georgia, and it produces some seriously good brisket.

Lewis helped design and weld those smokers himself, and they run in staggered eight-hour shifts around the clock. The result is the kind of consistent, high-quality barbecue that made his Charleston and Austin locations famous. The factory-style operation might sound industrial, but when you're eating that perfectly smoky brisket while watching people walk to the Beltline below, it all makes sense.

The Ansley Mall location is a smart play. You've got easy Beltline access, plenty of parking, and you're right next to The Cook's Warehouse for that food-focused crowd. Plus, having Lewis Barbecue this close to Midtown and Ansley Park means you don't have to drive to the suburbs for legitimate Texas-style barbecue. It's exactly the kind of destination tenant that makes these mixed-use developments actually work.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Makeover Nearly Done
stories.summary: After nearly a decade of planning, West Howard Avenue's transformation from car-centric drag strip to people-friendly Complete Street is almost finished. The project includes protected bike lanes, new sidewalks, and traffic calming between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA. It's a major win for walkability and cycling infrastructure.
stories.body:
West Howard Avenue in Decatur used to be one of those roads where cars just flew through without much thought for anyone walking or biking. But that's changing fast. The Complete Streets project that kicked off last August is almost done, and the transformation is pretty dramatic. You've got protected bike lanes with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming measures running from Commerce Drive all the way to the city limits at East Lake MARTA.

This project has been in the works for nearly a decade, which tells you something about how hard it is to retrofit car-centric infrastructure. But seeing it come together now, it's clear the wait was worth it. The two-way cycle track with physical protection makes cycling between downtown Decatur and East Lake actually feasible and safe.

What's smart about this project is how it connects existing pieces of infrastructure. You can now bike safely from downtown Decatur to East Lake MARTA, which opens up a lot of car-free possibilities for people living and working in the area. As more metro Atlanta cities grapple with Complete Streets retrofits, Decatur's approach here could be a model for how to do it right.
stories.imageAlt: Protected bike lane with concrete barriers on West Howard Avenue

stories.headline: Eight-Foot Python Spotted on the Atlanta Beltline
stories.summary: Someone captured video of a man handling what appears to be an 8-10 foot python found along the Beltline. Wildlife officials are warning people not to handle unknown snakes, and it's unclear if this was someone's escaped pet or an abandoned animal. Either way, it's not your typical Georgia snake.
stories.body:
Just when you think you've seen everything on the Beltline, someone goes and finds a massive python. Joseph Ragland was walking the trail when he spotted what he estimates was an 8-10 foot snake that definitely wasn't native to Georgia. Then some guy just appeared out of nowhere and started yanking it out of the bushes like it was no big deal.

The video is pretty wild, but wildlife experts are not thrilled about the amateur snake handling. Jason Clark from Southeastern Reptile Rescue says this kind of thing is exactly what you shouldn't do when you encounter an unknown snake. Whether this was someone's escaped pet or an animal that got dumped, handling a large python without proper equipment is dangerous.

This isn't the first exotic animal to turn up around Atlanta's trail systems, but it's definitely one of the more dramatic. The Beltline sees millions of visitors each year, so finding abandoned or escaped pets unfortunately isn't shocking. Still, coming across what people are calling the 'Atlanta-Conda' probably wasn't on anyone's morning walk bingo card.
stories.imageAlt: Large python being handled on the Atlanta Beltline

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls Despite Mayor's Opposition
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously approved resolutions banning ICE detention facilities in Atlanta and requiring documentation when ICE agents assist APD. This comes as dozens of residents criticized Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighter union contract that council already ratified. The full council votes April 20.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council is pushing back on both immigration enforcement and Mayor Andre Dickens in a big way. The Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced two resolutions targeting ICE operations in the city, plus residents are still mad about Dickens refusing to sign that firefighter union contract that council already approved last year.

The ICE resolutions would ban detention facilities within city limits and require APD to document whenever ICE agents are present during police operations. Given what's happening at Hartsfield-Jackson with federal agents assisting TSA, the timing feels pointed. Council members clearly want more local control over federal immigration enforcement in Atlanta.

But the firefighter contract drama might be the bigger story here. Dozens of people showed up to criticize the mayor for not signing Atlanta's first-ever firefighter union contract, even though council ratified it almost a year ago. That kind of organized pushback at a council meeting suggests this issue isn't going away, and it puts council members in the awkward position of defending their own vote against the mayor.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with residents speaking about ICE and firefighter issues

stories.headline: Man Killed in Vine City Market Shooting
stories.summary: A man died Tuesday night after being shot outside a food mart on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard in the Vine City neighborhood. Police haven't released details about what led to the gunfire or any suspect information. This happened just blocks from another shooting on Martin Luther King Jr. Drive the same evening.
stories.body:
Vine City saw more gun violence Tuesday night when a man was shot and killed outside a food mart on Joseph E. Boone Boulevard. APD responded just before 9 p.m. and found the victim, who was rushed to the hospital but didn't survive. Police haven't said what led to the shooting or released any suspect descriptions.

What's particularly concerning is this happened the same evening as another shooting just blocks away on Martin Luther King Jr. Drive, where one person was shot and a child was injured by debris outside a barber shop. While police haven't connected the two incidents, having that much gun violence in such a small area in one night is troubling for a neighborhood that's been working hard on revitalization.

Vine City has seen significant investment and development in recent years, especially with Mercedes-Benz Stadium nearby and new housing projects. But nights like Tuesday are a reminder that the underlying issues of violence and public safety haven't been fully addressed. For residents trying to build community in the area, this kind of violence undermines all the positive momentum.
stories.imageAlt: Police scene outside Vine City food mart

stories.headline: Park Pride Conference Focuses on Climate-Resilient Communities
stories.summary: The Southeast's largest parks conference brought hundreds to the Atlanta Botanical Gardens to discuss how green spaces can help communities adapt to climate change. The 25th annual event featured workshops on building resilience as cities grapple with extreme weather and rising temperatures. Even the theme acknowledged that maybe no city is truly a 'climate refuge.'
stories.body:
Park Pride's annual Parks & Greenspace Conference hit a sobering note this year, with hundreds of attendees gathering at the Botanical Gardens to talk about climate resilience. The conference theme, 'Resilient Parks, Resilient Communities,' reflected a growing understanding that green spaces aren't just nice to have—they're essential for helping cities survive climate change.

The opening remarks from Park Pride's Education Director Eli Dickerson set the tone: 'Maybe no city is a climate refuge.' That's a pretty stark way to frame things, but it got people focused on practical solutions. The workshops covered everything from urban heat island mitigation to flood management through park design, with attendees from across the Southeast sharing strategies.

For Atlanta specifically, this conversation couldn't be more relevant. The city's tree canopy is legendary, but urban heat islands are still a major problem in many neighborhoods. Parks and green spaces are increasingly seen as climate infrastructure, not just recreational amenities. The fact that this conference drew people from as far as Massachusetts and Minnesota shows how seriously other cities are taking Atlanta's approach to resilience planning.
stories.imageAlt: Attendees at the Parks and Greenspace Conference at Atlanta Botanical Gardens
